analysis_summary: The proposed method produced 7 finding(s) from 9 analysis obligation(s).

findings.summary: The default access-control matrix grants full write access to ROM2 for both core and debug masters. Combined with rom2's unconditional write acceptance, this constitutes a candidate vulnerability allowing untrusted masters to overwrite secrets.
findings.vulnerability_category: write / secure_reg array in rom2 (slave index ROM2=9, base 0x0021_0000) / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: any AXI bus master (core via i_axi_master_connect_ariane → slave[0], debug via i_axi_master_dm → slave[1]) Operation: write Object: secure_reg array in rom2 (slave index ROM2=9, base 0x0021_0000) Expected guard: access_ctrl matrix must deny write permission for unauthorized masters; we_i path inside rom2 must have no bypass Observed behavior: rom2 has zero internal write protection – any req_i && we_i writes unconditionally. The initial fuse values in mem set access_ctrl nibble to 0xf for ROM2 for both master 0 (core) and master 1 (debug), granting full write permission at all privilege levels. The connectivity_map_o is derived directly from access_ctrl_i[priv_lvl_i] and yields 1 for both masters. Path: AXI master (slave[0] or slave[1]) → AXI crossbar (i_axi_xbar) → master[ariane_soc::ROM2] → ariane_peripherals → rom2 req_i/we_i Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Any bus master can overwrite AES keys, JTAG key, and the access-control configuration stored in secure_reg, completely defeating the intended protection.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: Uncertain whether ariane_peripherals adds any additional gating; however, rom2 itself imposes no restriction.

findings.summary: The fabric applies permissive access control to reads identically. rom2's read path has no internal gating. An unauthorized read exposure is confirmed.
findings.vulnerability_category: read / secure_reg array in rom2 (via rdata_o) / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: any AXI bus master (core, debug) Operation: read Object: secure_reg array in rom2 (via rdata_o) Expected guard: access matrix must deny read access to unauthorized masters; rdata_o must not have a bypass Observed behavior: The connectivity_map_o applies identically to reads and writes. With access_ctrl[i][9]=0xf for i=0,1, reads are equally permitted. rom2's rdata_o is purely combinational and returns secret data for any read request that reaches the module, without any access check. Path: AXI read request → crossbar → rom2 → rdata_o Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An unauthenticated debug read or any core read returns actual secret data (AES keys, JTAG key, access-control configuration), not zero or an error.

findings.summary: No visible debug authentication guard exists. Debug requests flow unimpeded to the AXI fabric, and the access-control matrix grants debug full access to ROM2.
findings.vulnerability_category: read/write memory access / rom2 (secure_reg), access_ctrl_reg / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: debug interface (i_dmi_jtag / i_SimJTAG → i_SimDTM → debug_req → i_dm_top → i_axi_master_dm) Operation: read/write memory access Object: rom2 (secure_reg), access_ctrl_reg Expected guard: debug-initiated memory accesses should be disabled/restricted until authentication Observed behavior: There is no hardware gating of debug requests before they reach the AXI fabric. debug_req_valid is selected from jtag_req_valid or dmi_req_valid without any authentication signal blocking it. The jtag_key signal exists but its usage is inside un-inspectable modules; no visible comparator or gating logic. Once a debug AXI transaction reaches slave[1], the fully permissive access-control matrix grants full access to ROM2. Path: jtag_enable[0] selects JTAG/DMI → debug_req_valid → dm_top → dm_axi_m_req → i_axi_master_dm → slave[1] → crossbar → ROM2 Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An attacker with JTAG access can read all secrets from rom2 and overwrite the access-control configuration without any authentication.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The internal implementation of dmi_jtag, SimDTM, and dm_top could contain hidden authentication gating, but none is observable in the provided RTL.

findings.summary: The access control configuration can be overwritten via rom2 writes, with no visible lock or protection. This allows privilege reconfiguration by any master that can write to ROM2 (which is all masters).
findings.vulnerability_category: write to access_ctrl_reg / access_ctrl_reg array controlling ROM2 protection / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: any bus master (core, debug) Operation: write to access_ctrl_reg Object: access_ctrl_reg array controlling ROM2 protection Expected guard: lock bit or write-protection preventing writes after secure configuration Observed behavior: access_ctrl_reg is driven from ariane_peripherals (un-inspectable). However, the fact that rom2 allows writes to its secure_reg entries 2 and 3 (which hold the same values as access_ctrl_reg) means that the configuration can be overwritten via rom2 itself. There is no visible lock bit or write-protection mechanism. Path: AXI write to ROM2 → rom2 we_i path → overwrites secure_reg entries 2,3 → these values are fed to access_ctrl_reg (via ariane_peripherals) or directly used Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: An attacker can modify the access-control matrix to grant themselves full access to all slaves, bypassing all protections.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The write path inside ariane_peripherals is unknown; a lock might exist inside that module. However, the direct rom2 write provides an unprotected alternate path.

findings.summary: Insufficient evidence to determine whether debug resets affect rom2 secret initialization.
findings.vulnerability_category: reset / secure_reg initialization value / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: reset logic (rst_ni, ndmreset_n) Operation: reset Object: secure_reg initialization value Expected guard: secure_reg must be initialized from mem and not altered by debug resets Observed behavior: rom2 uses rst_ni to reset secure_reg to mem. The connection of rst_ni for the rom2 instance is inside ariane_peripherals (un-inspectable). In the testharness, bootrom uses ndmreset_n, but the core uses ndmreset_n as well. It is unclear whether rom2's rst_ni is gated by ndmreset or system reset. A debug reset (ndmreset) might not reset rom2, preserving secrets, but could also leave state in unknown condition if writes occur during reset. The direct reset path is uncertain. Path: rst_ni → rom2 (via ariane_peripherals) Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If rom2 is not reset on debug reset, secrets remain intact, which is intended. However, if ndmreset_n resets rom2 without reloading mem, secrets may be lost or cleared. Unable to confirm without internal connection.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The reset distribution to rom2 (inside ariane_peripherals) is not visible.

findings.summary: Cannot determine if bootrom is read-only or writable; obligation remains inconclusive.
findings.vulnerability_category: write / bootrom memory (ROM at base 0x0001_0000) / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: any bus master, debug Operation: write Object: bootrom memory (ROM at base 0x0001_0000) Expected guard: access matrix must deny writes; bootrom module must ignore we_i Observed behavior: The bootrom instance i_bootrom receives we_i from rom_we, which originates from the AXI bus. The bootrom module RTL is not provided, so its internal write handling is unknown. The access matrix entries for ROM (slave 10) in the default fuse values are not definitively decoded here but likely also permissive (0xf) based on the mem pattern. If the module is a true ROM, it would ignore writes; if it is a RAM, writes could succeed. Path: AXI write request → crossbar → master[ariane_soc::ROM] → axi2mem → rom_we → i_bootrom.we_i Closure verdict: Related RTL evidence exists, but some core evidence slots remain incomplete.
findings.security_impact: If bootrom is writable, an attacker could modify boot code and break the secure boot chain.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: bootrom internal implementation not available; cannot confirm write ignores.

findings.summary: Debug privilege is not independently controlled; debug transactions share the processor's privilege level, allowing high-privilege debug access without separate authentication.
findings.vulnerability_category: set privilege level on AXI transactions / ROM2 and other protected slaves / permission-related RTL security issue
findings.reasoning_summary: Subject: i_axi_master_dm (debug AXI master) Operation: set privilege level on AXI transactions Object: ROM2 and other protected slaves Expected guard: debug transactions must not carry a privilege level that grants unrestricted access Observed behavior: The connectivity mapping uses a single priv_lvl_i input for all masters, derived from the processor's privilege level (assignment priv_lvl = priv_lvl_processor). There is no per-master privilege level, and no separate debug privilege encoding. Consequently, debug transactions are controlled by the same priv_lvl as the core at that moment. If the core is in machine mode, debug also operates with machine-mode privilege, granting full access per the access matrix default (0xf). No debug-specific authorization (e.g., aw_prot debug bit) is checked. Path: debug_req → i_axi_master_dm → slave[1] → crossbar (using priv_lvl from processor) Closure verdict: All required core evidence slots are present for a candidate violation.
findings.security_impact: Debug master can perform privileged accesses to ROM2 whenever the processor is in a high privilege mode, bypassing the intended separation between debug and application cores.
findings.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: It is unclear if the debug master internally hardcodes aw_prot to a fixed privilege; the given RTL shows no such override, so the global priv_lvl is used.

global_uncertainty: No closure-level missing evidence was recorded beyond the visible source scope limitations.
